event: Neolithic communities settle the highlands and coastal plains of southern Arabia, developing early agriculture and animal domestication.
Sabaean settlers establish irrigated agriculture and begin organizing the polity centered on Marib in the fertile highland valleys.
The Kingdom of Saba (Sheba) rises as a dominant power, controlling the lucrative frankincense and myrrh trade routes.
Construction of the Great Dam of Marib begins, becoming an engineering marvel that irrigates vast agricultural lands.
The South Arabian script and Old South Arabian languages flourish across the emerging kingdoms of the region.
The Kingdom of Ma'in (Minaean kingdom) thrives as a major trading power along the incense routes.
The Himyarite Kingdom emerges in the highlands, gradually absorbing neighboring states and dominating southern Arabia.
A Roman expedition under Aelius Gallus marches into Arabia Felix but fails to conquer the prosperous southern kingdoms.
The Himyarite Kingdom unifies most of southern Arabia, including Saba and Hadramawt, under a single rule.
Himyarite rulers adopt monotheism, with many embracing Judaism as the kingdom's official religion.
The Christian Kingdom of Aksum invades and conquers Himyar, ending Jewish Himyarite rule after the persecution of Christians.
Sasanian Persia intervenes and seizes control of Yemen, establishing it as a satrapy of the Persian Empire.
The Great Dam of Marib finally collapses after centuries of decline, triggering migration and the end of an era of prosperity.
Yemen's Persian governor Badhan converts to Islam, peacefully bringing the region into the early Muslim community.
Imam al-Hadi ila'l-Haqq Yahya founds the Zaydi Shia imamate in Sa'dah, beginning a religious-political institution that endures for over a millennium.
The Sulayhid dynasty, an Ismaili Shia state, rises to power and unifies much of Yemen under its rule.
Queen Arwa al-Sulayhi begins her long and celebrated reign, becoming one of the most renowned female rulers in Islamic history.
Turan-Shah, brother of Saladin, leads an Ayyubid conquest of Yemen, bringing the region under Egyptian-based rule.
The Rasulid dynasty establishes independent rule over Yemen, ushering in a golden age of prosperity, scholarship, and architecture.
The Tahirid dynasty supplants the declining Rasulids, ruling much of Yemen from their capital cities.
Mamluk forces seize parts of Yemen shortly before the Ottoman conquest of Egypt reshapes regional power.
The Ottoman Empire begins its first occupation of Yemen, establishing the Eyalet of Yemen to control the Red Sea coast.
Imam al-Mansur al-Qasim launches a Zaydi resistance movement against Ottoman rule, founding the Qasimid dynasty.
The Qasimid Zaydi imams expel the Ottomans, establishing an independent Yemeni state that controls the coffee trade.
Mocha (al-Mukha) flourishes as the world's principal coffee-exporting port, giving its name to mocha coffee.
Britain captures the port of Aden, establishing a strategic coaling station and colonial foothold on the route to India.
The Ottoman Empire returns to Yemen, gradually reasserting control over the northern highlands and Tihama coast.
The opening of the Suez Canal dramatically increases Aden's strategic and commercial importance to the British Empire.
Imam Yahya Hamid ad-Din assumes leadership of the Zaydi imamate and intensifies resistance to Ottoman rule.
With the Ottoman Empire's collapse after World War I, Imam Yahya establishes the independent Kingdom of Yemen in the north.
The Treaty of Taif ends a brief war with Saudi Arabia, fixing the northern border after Yemeni territorial losses.
Imam Yahya is assassinated in a failed coup, and his son Ahmad takes power as the new ruler of the kingdom.
Republican officers overthrow the monarchy and proclaim the Yemen Arab Republic, sparking a civil war backed by Egypt and Saudi Arabia.
Britain withdraws from Aden and the People's Republic of South Yemen gains independence after a fierce anti-colonial struggle.
South Yemen becomes the People's Democratic Republic of Yemen, the Arab world's only Marxist state, aligned with the Soviet Union.
Border clashes erupt between North and South Yemen, followed by agreements that nominally aim at eventual unification.
Ali Abdullah Saleh becomes president of the Yemen Arab Republic, beginning a decades-long dominance of Yemeni politics.
A violent power struggle within South Yemen's ruling party erupts into a brief but bloody civil war in Aden.
North and South Yemen unite to form the Republic of Yemen, with Ali Abdullah Saleh as president and Sana'a as capital.
A civil war between northern and southern forces ends with the defeat of southern secessionists and consolidation of unity.
Al-Qaeda bombs the USS Cole in Aden harbor, killing seventeen sailors and highlighting Yemen's role in global terrorism concerns.
The Houthi insurgency begins in the northern Sa'dah province, igniting years of conflict with the central government.
Arab Spring protests force President Saleh to step down after months of mass demonstrations and political crisis.
Abdrabbuh Mansur Hadi becomes president in a transitional election following Saleh's resignation under a GCC-brokered deal.
Houthi forces seize the capital Sana'a, plunging the country toward full-scale civil war and state collapse.
A Saudi-led coalition launches a military intervention against the Houthis, escalating the war and a catastrophic humanitarian crisis.
The Stockholm Agreement brokers a ceasefire around the vital port city of Hodeidah amid widespread famine warnings.
A UN-brokered truce brings a significant reduction in fighting, and a Presidential Leadership Council replaces President Hadi.
Houthi forces begin attacks on Red Sea shipping in response to the Gaza war, disrupting global maritime trade.
